title: Eggs Benedict
subtitle: Poached eggs and Canadian bacon on English muffins, with hollandaise
description: Eggs Benedict is the brunch standard: a split, toasted English muffin topped with Canadian bacon, a perfectly poached egg, and a generous ladle of hollandaise sauce. It is a dish built from technique rather than ingredient complexity — the poached egg and hollandaise both require some attention and a thermometer is helpful for the sauce. Made methodically, in the right order, the whole thing comes together in about thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: Eggs Benedict is attributed to multiple New York sources from the late nineteenth century — most commonly to Lemuel Benedict, a Wall Street broker who ordered the dish at the Waldorf Hotel around 1894. The dish appears in Oscar Tschirky's The Cook Book by 'Oscar' of the Waldorf (1896).

recipeTools.notes: For the hollandaise — used as a bain-marie over another pan of simmering water.
For poaching the eggs — wide and deep enough to hold 4 cm of water.
For lifting poached eggs from the water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A warm, emulsified butter sauce made from egg yolks and clarified or whole butter, flavoured with lemon juice and white wine vinegar. One of the five French mother sauces, and the defining sauce of eggs Benedict.
Cooking eggs without the shell in water kept just below a simmer (85–90 °C). Acidulated water (with a splash of vinegar) helps the white set around the yolk cleanly.

ingredients.notes: The freshest available — fresh whites hold together better when poached
Or good cooked ham — 2 slices per serving

steps: Make the hollandaise: set a heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water (the bowl should not touch the water). Add the egg yolks, lemon juice, and white wine vinegar. Whisk constantly until the mixture thickens and leaves a ribbon when the whisk is lifted — about 3–4 minutes. The bowl should be warm but not hot; if it gets too hot, remove from the water briefly.

Remove from the heat. Begin adding the warm melted butter in a thin, steady drizzle, whisking constantly. The sauce should thicken and become glossy as you add the butter. If it gets too thick, add a teaspoon of warm water. Season with salt and a pinch of cayenne. Keep warm over the pan of hot water with the heat off.
Fill a wide saucepan with water to a 4 cm depth and bring to a bare simmer. Add the white wine vinegar.
Toast the muffins and warm the Canadian bacon in a dry pan.

Poach the eggs: crack each egg into a small cup. Create a gentle swirl in the water with a spoon. Slide the eggs into the water one at a time. Poach for 3 minutes for a runny yolk, 4 minutes for a slightly firmer one. Lift with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper.

To assemble: place a toasted muffin half on each plate. Top with two slices of Canadian bacon. Place a poached egg on top of the bacon. Spoon hollandaise generously over the egg. Garnish with parsley or chives. Serve immediately.

content.text: first — it keeps warm over the pan of hot water with the heat off while you poach the eggs. The eggs are last: hot, fresh, and drained, they go straight onto the assembled plate and the sauce goes over immediately. Organisation is the skill here.

Set a heatproof bowl over a pan of barely simmering water. Add {{egg-yolk}} of egg yolks, the lemon juice, and white wine vinegar. Whisk constantly for 3–4 minutes until thickened and ribbon-like. Remove from heat and drizzle in {{butter-unsalted}} of warm melted butter in a thin stream, whisking constantly. Season with salt and cayenne. Keep warm over hot water with the heat off.
Bring a wide pan of water to a bare simmer with a splash of vinegar added. Toast the muffins and warm the bacon.

{{egg}} of eggs: crack each into a small cup, create a gentle swirl in the water, and slide the eggs in. Cook 3 minutes for runny, 4 for slightly set. Drain on kitchen paper. Place a muffin half on each plate, top with bacon, then the poached egg, then a generous spoonful of hollandaise. Garnish and serve immediately.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The two most common technical failures.

attrs.problem: Hollandaise has split into an oily, curdled mess
attrs.solution: The butter was added too fast, or the bowl got too hot. To rescue: start with a new yolk in a clean bowl and very slowly whisk the split sauce into it, as if making a new hollandaise with the split sauce as the 'butter'.

attrs.problem: Poached egg white is spreading thin and ragged
attrs.solution: The eggs were not fresh enough, the water was boiling rather than barely simmering, or the vinegar was omitted. Use the freshest eggs available — their whites are tighter. The water should register around 85–90 °C, not a rolling boil.
